notes: Daily missile and drone series here are non-cumulative and based on the best complete public day-by-day reconstruction available through Mar 9.
Latest target-country charts are daily slices, not cumulative totals, because daily change is the useful signal for this dashboard.
As of Mar 13, post-Mar 9 public reporting is stronger on oil, shipping, and country-level incident fragments than on a clean new all-theater daily launch ledger.

metrics.desc: Mar 09 daily drones in best complete public series

metrics.label: Current layer
metrics.desc: Oil, shipping, and incident fragments updated through today

sitrep: The best complete public daily launch series still ends on March 9 and shows a sharp decline from the opening phase.
As of March 13, the newest hard signal is not a fresh full daily launch ledger but continued oil and shipping stress, plus additional country-level incident fragments.
That means the core theory should currently be judged with two layers together: the falling daily launch curve through March 9 and the still-elevated cost pressure visible on March 13.

theoryEvaluation.title: Support for the theory
theoryEvaluation.body: The best complete public day-by-day launch series still supports the claim that meaningful missile and drone launch capability has been degraded materially from the opening phase.

theoryEvaluation.title: What weakens confidence
theoryEvaluation.body: Public post-March 9 launch reporting remains fragmented, so current evidence does not yet prove from public data alone that capability has been fully broken rather than reduced to a lower-volume adaptive phase.

theoryEvaluation.title: Current judgment as of Mar 13
theoryEvaluation.body: The latest available evidence supports capability degraded materially more strongly than capability destroyed. Meanwhile, oil and shipping disruption remain high enough to keep political and economic costs strategically relevant.

scenarioProbabilities.name: Degraded but still dangerous; messy de-escalation
scenarioProbabilities.body: Launch tempo remains well below the opening phase, but residual capability and regional disruption persist long enough to produce a negotiated or pressured wind-down rather than a clean decisive finish.

scenarioProbabilities.name: Further suppression enables stronger coercive pressure
scenarioProbabilities.body: Missile and drone capability keeps eroding faster than political and economic costs rise, giving Washington and Tel Aviv more room to intensify pressure and claim operational victory.

scenarioProbabilities.name: Conflict drags with periodic flare-ups
scenarioProbabilities.body: Residual launch capability, proxy activity, and maritime disruption keep the conflict alive at lower intensity even without a return to opening-day barrage levels.

scenarioProbabilities.name: Cost shock forces early strategic pullback
scenarioProbabilities.body: Oil, shipping, allied exposure, or domestic politics force a pause before launch capability is decisively broken.

dailySeries.sourceLabel: Best complete public day-by-day series currently loaded; no equally complete public all-theater daily extension beyond Mar 09 was confirmed today

oil.note: Brent was about $100.13 on Mar 13 after peaking at $119.50 earlier in the week; WTI current layer is kept at about $96.00. Gulf supply and shipping disruption remain the key cost-side signal.

latestTargetBreakdown.note: Latest daily slices available; zeros may mean no public hit recorded in the loaded slice, not guaranteed inactivity.

dailyRows.capability: Opening surge shows full usable inventory and launch-chain readiness.
dailyRows.cost: Political shock and oil risk begin but remain secondary to battlefield surprise.

dailyRows.capability: Peak intensity suggests launch architecture still largely intact.
dailyRows.cost: Regional anxiety expands; oil and shipping sensitivity rise.

dailyRows.capability: Still high, but no longer climbing; first hints of attrition or recalibration.
dailyRows.cost: Broader target spread increases regional pressure and insurance risk.

dailyRows.capability: Sustained but sliding, consistent with growing suppression or logistics strain.
dailyRows.cost: Wider regional profile makes economic and diplomatic costs harder to contain.

dailyRows.capability: Meaningful degradation becoming visible; lower-volume pressure still possible.
dailyRows.cost: Oil and shipping signals start to matter more relative to launch count.

dailyRows.capability: Mass strike capacity appears weaker; residual capacity remains.
dailyRows.cost: Costs now have a clearer path to influence coalition decision-making.

dailyRows.capability: Selective rather than mass launch behavior points to either depletion, suppression, or both.
dailyRows.cost: Shipping and oil stress are now strategic, not background noise.

dailyRows.capability: Daily usable launch throughput looks substantially reduced.
dailyRows.cost: If energy disruption persists, coalition patience becomes more testable.

dailyRows.capability: Residual launch pressure survives despite heavy attrition.
dailyRows.cost: Cost side matters more because battlefield tempo is no longer dominating headlines.

dailyRows.capability: Near-floor daily output in the loaded series; does not prove complete destruction.
dailyRows.cost: Decision pressure now depends heavily on oil, shipping, and politics.

timeline.title: Immediate horizon (next 72 hours)
timeline.body: Best watched through oil, shipping, and target spread because public full daily launch data remains patchier than in the opening phase.

timeline.title: Near-term horizon (next 4–10 days)
timeline.body: If no rebound in daily launch tempo appears and target-country spread narrows, the attrition case strengthens.

timeline.title: Decision horizon (next 1–3 weeks)
timeline.body: The war’s direction likely turns on whether coalition decision-makers judge launch suppression sufficient before cost pressure becomes politically intolerable.

timeline.title: Main uncertainty
timeline.body: A major mass-casualty strike, wider proxy activation, or persistent Hormuz disruption could yank the timeline off its current path.

endstate.title: Most likely
endstate.body: A pressured de-escalation after meaningful attrition of launch capability, with both sides claiming success.

endstate.title: Theory-aligned scenario
endstate.body: If missile and drone capability is broken before cost pressure forces a stop, coercive pressure on regime leadership rises sharply.

endstate.title: What would change this
endstate.body: Evidence of restored launch volume, slower-than-expected attrition, or political and economic pressure forcing an early operational pause.

prepIranfarhang: Rank current outbound and inbound orders by institutional importance and time sensitivity.
Batch low-priority titles more aggressively and protect periodicals and subscription continuity first.
Prepare customer-facing delay language that is compliance-conscious and operationally specific.
Keep duplicate metadata, invoices, and packing records outside Iran to preserve service continuity.
signalsIranfarhang: Iranian outbound air-freight disruption lasting more than several days.
Repeated customs holds or manual inspections affecting informational-material shipments.
University or library procurement teams asking for heightened sanctions review or pausing approvals.
Breakdown in Atlanta replenishment cadence or inability to maintain expected batching windows.
prepCorridor: Map each payment flow to its bank, exchange house, and beneficial-owner exposure.
Define backup settlement paths and backup ports before you need them.
Shorten receivable cycles and model working-capital stress under 7, 14, and 30-day disruption cases.
Pre-write counterparty documentation packs for fast compliance responses.
signalsCorridor: UAE bank account closures or widened transaction rejections for Iran-linked clients.
Exchange-house restrictions, monitoring crackdowns, or licensing actions.
Jebel Ali or related Gulf cargo holds, reroutes, or documentation surges.
Shipping notices shifting liability or forcing end-of-voyage changes mid-route.
